name: Advanced Code Review
description: Senior-level PR review scenarios — architecture, performance, and real-world tradeoffs

questions.question: What race condition exists in this custom hook?
options: Fast typing can cause out-of-order responses — an earlier request may resolve after a later one, showing stale results
The useState initializer runs on every render causing unnecessary allocations
The fetch call is missing a Content-Type header which will cause CORS failures
The dependency array should include setResults to avoid stale closure issues
questions.explanation: Without an AbortController or a request ID check, rapid query changes can cause responses to arrive out of order. The last-fired request may not be the last to resolve, so stale results overwrite fresh ones. The fix is to use an AbortController in the useEffect cleanup, or track a request sequence number.

questions.question: What stability issue would you flag in this custom hook's return value?
options: The initialValue parameter should be wrapped in useRef to prevent reinitialization
onChange and validate are recreated every render, causing unnecessary re-renders in consumers that use them as deps or pass them to memoized children
The hook should use useReducer instead of two separate useState calls
setError(null) in onChange should be debounced to avoid excessive state updates
questions.explanation: onChange and validate are new function references on every render. Any component using these in a useEffect dependency array or passing them to React.memo children will re-render unnecessarily. Wrapping them in useCallback fixes referential stability. This is a critical review catch for shared hooks.

questions.question: What is the most critical issue in this API layer design?
options: The function should use XMLHttpRequest for better browser compatibility
Error handling throws a generic Error losing the response body — the caller can't distinguish between a 401 (needs re-auth), 404 (not found), and 500 (server error) to handle each appropriately
Using template literals for URL construction is a security risk
The Content-Type header should only be set for POST/PUT requests
questions.explanation: Throwing a generic Error with just the status code discards the response body and makes it impossible for callers to implement proper error recovery. A 401 should trigger token refresh, a 422 should show validation errors, a 429 should trigger retry-after. Senior reviewers flag this because poor error discrimination cascades into bad UX throughout the entire app.

questions.question: What architectural concern would you raise in your review?
options: The progress state should use useRef instead of useState to avoid re-renders

Multiple boolean flags create impossible states — the component needs a state machine (e.g., idle | uploading | retrying | success | error) to prevent bugs like isUploading + isSuccess both being true
The handler should be wrapped in useCallback since it's likely passed as a prop
useState should be replaced with useReducer for better performance with many state values
questions.explanation: Four independent booleans create 2^4 = 16 possible states, most of which are invalid (e.g., uploading AND success AND error simultaneously). A single status enum or state machine like XState makes impossible states unrepresentable. This is one of the most common senior-level review catches — boolean flag soup always leads to subtle bugs as requirements grow.

questions.question: What bundle size concern would you flag in this PR?
options: Named exports are slower than default exports and should be avoided
Barrel exports can defeat tree-shaking — importing just Button may pull in DataGrid, RichTextEditor, and ChartWidget into the bundle depending on the bundler configuration
The @ path alias adds overhead compared to relative imports
Each component should be in its own npm package for proper code splitting
questions.explanation: Barrel files (index.js re-exports) can break tree-shaking in Webpack 4 and some configurations of Webpack 5. Importing Button may cause the bundler to include the entire module graph of all exported components, including heavy ones like RichTextEditor and ChartWidget. The fix is direct imports (import { Button } from '@/components/Button') or configuring sideEffects: false in package.json.

questions.question: What performance anti-pattern would you flag in this PR?
options: useQuery should be replaced with useSWR for better caching behavior
N+1 request waterfall — rendering 20 members fires 20 individual stats requests sequentially, and they can't even start until the members query resolves
The team and members queries should be combined into a single query to reduce re-renders
MemberCard should be wrapped in React.memo to prevent re-renders when stats update
questions.explanation: This is the frontend equivalent of an N+1 database query. The stats requests can't start until members load (waterfall), and then each member triggers its own HTTP request. With 50 members, that's 52 sequential network calls. The fix is a batch endpoint (fetchAllMemberStats(teamId)) or a GraphQL query that fetches everything in one request.

questions.question: What migration concern would you raise in review?
options: The function should accept a version parameter instead of hardcoding v2

There is no fallback to v1 — if v2 isn't deployed to all environments yet, or the rollout is gradual, this change will break users on older backends. The migration needs a transition period supporting both APIs
The locale field mapping is incorrect — 'en-US' is not the same as 'en'
The function should validate the response shape with a runtime type check like Zod
questions.explanation: API migrations need backwards compatibility during the transition. If the backend rolls out v2 gradually, or if the frontend deploys before the backend, users will get errors. A safe migration pattern: try v2 first, fall back to v1, log which version was used. Once v2 adoption hits 100%, remove the v1 fallback in a follow-up PR.

questions.question: What would you flag about this error boundary implementation?
options: ErrorBoundary should be a class component — function components can't catch errors
A single top-level error boundary means any crash in any route kills the entire app with no way to recover — each route (or critical section) should have its own boundary with route-specific recovery
The fallback should use a Suspense boundary instead for better loading states
Error boundaries don't work with React Router and will cause hydration mismatches
questions.explanation: One error boundary for the whole app means a crash in the Reports page also takes down Dashboard and Settings. Users lose all functionality. Granular error boundaries per route or feature let the rest of the app keep working. The fallback should also include a retry mechanism (remount the route) rather than a dead-end error message.

questions.question: What would you flag in this shared component's API?
options: The component should use generic types instead of explicit prop definitions
The API surface is too large — 17 props creates high maintenance burden and makes the component hard to use correctly. It should be split into composition-based patterns or several focused variants
Using ReactNode for actions is too permissive and should be typed more strictly
The component needs a ref forwarding prop for imperative handle access
questions.explanation: A component with 17 props is a code smell in a shared library. It signals the component is trying to handle too many use cases, making it hard to maintain, document, and use correctly. Better patterns: compound components (Card.Image, Card.Actions), variant-specific wrappers (LinkCard, SelectableCard), or composition slots. The prop count directly correlates with bug surface area in a shared library.

questions.question: What security vulnerability would you flag in this PR?
options: The API endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection via the postId parameter

Using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-generated comment content is a stored XSS vulnerability — an attacker can inject malicious scripts via comment body that execute for every user viewing the post
The fetch call should use POST instead of GET to prevent CSRF attacks
The author field should be escaped to prevent clickjacking attacks
questions.explanation: dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-generated content (c.body) is a textbook stored XSS vulnerability. An attacker posts a comment containing <script>stealCookies()</script>, which then executes for every user who views that post. The fix is to use a sanitization library (like DOMPurify) before rendering, or better yet, render markdown instead of raw HTML.

questions.scenario:
```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml PR diff:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
-     - run: npm test
      - run: npx serverless deploy --stage production
+     - run: npm test
```
questions.question: What CI/CD issue would you flag in this config change?
options: The checkout action should be pinned to a specific SHA instead of a version tag
Tests were moved to run AFTER the production deploy — if tests fail, broken code is already in production. Tests must run before deploy to serve as a gate
npm ci should be replaced with npm install for faster dependency resolution
The serverless deploy command should use a matrix strategy for parallel deployments
questions.explanation: Moving the test step after deploy means broken code ships to production before tests catch it. This is a critical review catch — CI pipeline ordering defines your safety guarantees. Tests must always be a gate that blocks deployment, not a post-deploy verification. This subtle reordering might look like a simple cleanup but fundamentally breaks the deployment safety model.

questions.scenario:
```sql
-- Migration PR: Add user preferences table
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN preferences JSONB DEFAULT '{}';

-- New query used by the frontend:
SELECT u.*, p.team_name
FROM users u
JOIN projects p ON p.owner_id = u.id
WHERE u.preferences->>'theme' = 'dark';
```
questions.question: What would you flag reviewing this schema change from a frontend perspective?
options: JSONB columns are not supported by most ORMs and will cause type errors in the frontend
The query filters on a JSONB field without an index — this will be a full table scan that gets progressively slower as users grow, eventually causing frontend timeouts on pages that use this data
The JOIN should use a LEFT JOIN to handle users without projects
The DEFAULT '{}' should be DEFAULT NULL to save storage space
questions.explanation: Filtering on preferences->>'theme' without a GIN or expression index means a full sequential scan of the users table. With 10K users this is fine; with 1M users, this query will time out and your frontend will show spinners forever. As a frontend reviewer, you should flag: 'Does this query have an index? What's the expected table size?' A missing index on a frequently-queried field is a production incident waiting to happen.

questions.question: What memoization anti-pattern would you flag?
options: useMemo should be replaced with useCallback for the function values
Two of the three useMemos are wasteful — memoizing a static object literal and a pure function with no deps adds complexity and memory overhead without any render savings. Only the sort is justified
All three useMemos are necessary because React re-renders on every prop change
The products dependency should be a deep comparison using a custom hook
questions.explanation: formatPrice wraps a pure function with no dependencies — it will never change and the useMemo just adds overhead. containerStyle memoizes a static object — move it outside the component as a constant. Only sortedProducts benefits from memoization since sorting is O(n log n). Over-memoization is a common code smell: it adds cognitive complexity, memory overhead, and signals the author doesn't understand when memoization actually helps.

questions.question: What feature flag implementation issue would you flag?
options: The analytics tracking should be in the PricingPage component, not the enabler function

Feature flags stored in localStorage are not server-controlled — they can't be toggled remotely for incidents, they persist across sessions unexpectedly, and users can enable any flag via DevTools. Use a proper feature flag service with server-side evaluation
The flag should use a boolean instead of a string comparison
NewPricingLayout and LegacyPricingLayout should be lazy-loaded with React.lazy
questions.explanation: Client-side localStorage flags have serious problems: (1) Can't kill a broken feature remotely during an incident — you have to deploy a code fix. (2) Users can toggle any flag via DevTools, bypassing rollout controls. (3) Flags persist across sessions, making gradual rollouts unreliable. (4) No way to target specific user segments. Use a feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, etc.) with server-side evaluation and real-time kill switches.

questions.question: What internationalization (i18n) issues would you flag in this component?
options: The component should accept a language prop to support multiple locales

Multiple i18n violations: hardcoded English strings ('Add to cart', 'Out of stock', 'left'), currency formatted as raw string instead of Intl.NumberFormat, number display not locale-aware, and no RTL layout consideration. All user-visible strings need translation keys
The className strings should be locale-specific for proper styling
The Product type should have locale-specific fields for name and description
questions.explanation: This component has at least 4 i18n issues: (1) 'Add to cart', 'Out of stock', and 'left' are hardcoded English — use t('addToCart') translation keys. (2) Currency formatting (product.currency + price) breaks for currencies with different symbol positions (¥100 vs $100 vs 100€). Use Intl.NumberFormat. (3) Number formatting (product.stock) should be locale-aware (1,000 vs 1.000). (4) The layout may break in RTL languages. Senior reviewers catch these because fixing i18n retroactively is extremely expensive.
